Transcrição Completa: 4 Lessons From Going to 120k a Month in 4 Months with Nick Metzger
[00:00] "All right. To start this off, Huhi does a has a done with you offer. Does anybody like consulting? You will obviously. Yeah. Okay. Cool."
[00:12] "So, the last last mastermind presentation title was how we went from zero to 100K a month in three months and then back to zero the next. That was the title. And now I'm extending that title a bit to and then back to 120K in four months."
[00:30] "Okay. And we're aiming, as Jeremy said, for about like 150 180 this month. Uh we're on track for that. And I'm going to repeat my background story a little bit because it's important."
[00:43] "Last mastermind, for those who were here, you know it. Um for those who weren't here, you get to know it now. Um I started doing parkour when I was like, I don't know, 12, 13 years old. And we filmed that shit like every single day."
[01:00] "We were out training, uploading it to YouTube, obviously. And at some point in 2014, I had a tumor in my back and I couldn't train anymore, right? But I had this big goal of becoming a stuntman, moving to LA."
[01:18] "Who lived in LA? You lived. You did. Okay. So, I wanted I wanted to live there. That was my dream, right? I wanted to become a stunt man and work in Hollywood."
[01:30] "Um, but obviously because of the tumor that dream got shattered pretty much. So, I thought, hey, let's just shoot videos instead of doing parkour then because I enjoyed that kind of and and was like, hey, that's then I still get to keep my old friends obviously."
[01:48] "Um, so I did that and then in 2015 there was uh obviously everybody knows Sony, right, the company. So, they announced a competition for a new camera that they announced on the market."
[02:02] "It was like a Sony FS7 was the name and it's like a $10,000 12,000 camera at that time. and they said, 'Hey, do a short film about the topic inspiration.' And the best film obviously gets this camera."
[02:18] "And I thought, 'Hey, let's just give it a shot. It's a nice portfolio video. If if I don't win, obviously, and if I win, that would be great.' So, I gave it a shot."
[02:30] "And they had like this live webinar where they announced the top 10. And they went through 10, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, and they announced me as second place."
[02:42] "And I was like, 'Fuck, this this shit sucks.' It's like obviously it's awesome, but at the same time like the winner gets the camera. I don't get shit right?"
[02:53] "Five days later, Sony calls me and they're like, 'Hen Nick, we have news for you.' I'm like, 'Yeah, what what's up?' They say, 'Yeah, so the winner of the competition, he actually got disqualified because you didn't follow the rules, which means you get to get the camera.'"
[03:10] "And I'm like, 'Whoa, fuck yeah.' Because like at that time, I had like zero dollars in my bank account, right? and you suddenly get a 10,000 122,000 camera like that and that's for for a videographer I mean ask Jay afterwards that's like the best that could ever happen because we are just like tech tech nerds."
[03:32] "So obviously I had a lot of motivation um got to shoot a quick video like a three-minute video for a fire department because they were looking for more people and that video went viral got like 700,000 views on YouTube."
[03:47] "And they uh got 25 new applications for their apartment and I was like this shit is awesome. I make money with what I'm loving and I solve other people's problems and that is what really motivated me to keep going."
[04:02] "Like I get paid to sell solve people's problems and that's what you guys all do, right? And that is what it all comes down to and you will see why later."
[04:12] "So then I founded my company Venture Sum which is my video agency. I'm still in the process of selling it. I was expecting to sell it until end of March. It didn't happen. Out of seven buyers like six backed out. I have one left."
[04:28] "Um, so wish me luck with that. Uh, and then quickly realized that most of our clients that do videos, they have no clue about marketing. Like they don't know shit right?"
[04:40] "So I thought, okay, um, let's help them. And that was at the time when I got in touch with Jeremy, uh, by buying the DMM and I started my company, like the second company, uh, called Moneyree, uh, because I wanted to generate more money for people. That's literally how I came up with that name."
[05:00] "And um worked with car dealerships and had this big goal like always this big dream of okay I want to make 100k a month and I told that to Jeremy and that was when when he was like okay so instead of instead of instead of you making that 100k instead of you making that 100k in like three years how can you do it in three months."
[05:22] "That was like his question like how can you do that faster and that question stuck with me since then like all the time I'm asking myself okay how can I get there faster and that is one of the key lessons here."
[05:35] "Like ask yourself how can I achieve what I want to achieve in like half the time and then if you come up with the solution ask yourself again oh well how can I achieve it in half of half the time right that's key."
[05:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3 Things I Learned From Going 0 to 100K+ a Month with a New Offer 2 Times"
NÚMEROS: 0 to 100K+, 2 Times, Million/Month 2023
VISUAL: Slide de título principal com três pontos principais destacados, fundo escuro com texto branco, layout limpo e profissional
[05:55] "So today I'm going to be sharing with you the three things I learned from going zero to 100k plus a month with a new offer two times and how we plan to get to a million a month in 2023."
[06:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Learning #1: Listen to What the Successful Say"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Slide com título grande, subtítulo sobre seguir conselhos de pessoas bem-sucedidas, design minimalista
[06:15] "So learning number one is to me listen to what the successful say. Listen to what the people who are already there or beyond that tell you. Okay."
[06:25] "So for me a big inspiration uh regarding that learning definitely is Alex Hermosi. I'm glad that Ran isn't here because he would just have punched me in the face."
[06:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The 1-1-1 Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 1-1-1
VISUAL: Três colunas verticais mostrando: "1 Target Audience", "1 Offer", "1 Sales Process", design simétrico com ícones representando cada elemento
[06:40] "Um, and what I got from him was the 111 strategy. Okay, simple is king, but be very, very specific. What does that mean? Our offer is tailored to one target audience. It's literally one offer and it's one sales process."
[06:58] "Like, it's simple as fuck okay? We don't over complicate that stuff. It's literally basic stuff. It's super easy."
[07:08] "So, regarding the target audience, and that's what I mean by being specific, like most business owners, they would say, 'Yeah, our target audience are videographers, like if they even go that far, right?'"
[07:21] "Most business owners probably would say, 'Yeah, our target audience are business owners.' But that's not how it works. Okay?"
[07:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Target Audience Specificity"
NÚMEROS: 10K+ per month
VISUAL: Funil visual mostrando refinamento progressivo: "Videographers" → "Want to Grow Business" → "To 10K+ per Month", setas indicando estreitamento do foco
[07:33] "So, we pick videographers, but then be a bit more specific. So, it's videographers who want to grow their business, right? But how can we be a bit more specific? Videographers who want to grow their business to 10K plus a month because they're not there yet."
[07:50] "Okay, that is how we found our audience. We did the same thing for the offer."
[07:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Offer Structure"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado diretamente
VISUAL: Estrutura hierárquica mostrando "Consulting" no topo, ramificando para diferentes tipos: "1-on-1", "Group", "Masterminds", com destaque visual no "Group Consulting"
[08:00] "The offer first was let's do consulting because they don't have the money to spend on ads yet. They don't have the money to hire an agency yet. So, they need somebody who tells them how they how they can actually grow their business."
[08:15] "And it's super interesting like videographers are in regards to business, they don't know shit okay? They're still sending emails from an email address. They're not using a calendar to plan their days like they're not doing the basic stuff."
[08:30] "Okay, so we need to teach them that too. So I wanted to make it more specific. Thought okay consulting. What kind of consulting? I mean Jeremy is doing one-on-one plus group consulting plus masterminds."
[08:43] "And to me, the the most like ideal scenario obviously is the group consulting because think about it when he does these Saturday calls which bring everybody here a shit ton of value like he needs one hour for these, right?"
[08:58] "It's not like he needs one hour for every single one of us. And that makes it obviously attractive for for him, makes it attractive for me with my offer."
[09:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Group Consulting Components"
NÚMEROS: 3 weekly calls, 5 calls (upgrade), 7 days support
VISUAL: Quatro caixas mostrando componentes: "Video Training Base", "3 Weekly Live Calls", "Upgrading to 5 Calls", "7 Days WhatsApp Support", ícones de vídeo, calendário e chat
[09:12] "And to be even more specific with that group consulting thing and that's going to be interesting for you guys um is what we do is a video training as the base. Okay, it's literally like the DMM in example um but way way more uh step by step."
[09:30] "So literally literally like hey go to this website, click this button, click this button, type in this username, click this button, click this button. Literally like you couldn't get it wrong."
[09:42] "Our mindset for that video training was how can we give it to a monkey and he gets to 10k a month. And that's how we built it."
[09:51] "Then they get three weekly live calls. And in the meantime, we're actually upgrading it uh to five weekly live calls because we have more and more Australian clients. And you know the time zone difference, right? It's horrible."
[10:08] "Um a lot of our clients currently get up at 3:00 a.m. to join the live calls. So we're switching that up a bit. Uh these calls are here to for them to ask questions. Okay?"
[10:20] "So they can ask us questions and if if the questions run out, we pretty much pick every single buddy and go like, 'Hey, how's it working for you? How is it going for you? How is it going for you? How is it going for you?'"
[10:32] "Like we force them into talking to us because if we don't, then obviously nothing is going to happen. Okay?"
[10:40] "And then in addition to that, we do seven days a week WhatsApp support. So every single student of ours has their own one-on-one chat with me and my team, and they can literally ask us anything they want, but we also push them in these chats."
[10:55] "And I'm going to get to that at a later point. And then so that's uh target audience, one offer and now the one sales process."
[11:05] "In the end of this presentation, I'm going to uh give you the QR code again to the framework. So if I forget it, please remind me."
[11:13] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "One Sales Process: Instagram DM Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 2K to 7K followers, 120K last month
VISUAL: Fluxograma mostrando: "Daily Posts (Value)" → "Daily Stories (Lifestyle)" → "DM Strategy" → "Appointments", ícone do Instagram, setas conectando elementos
[11:16] "Um this is for now. The reason why we picked one sales process was like I wanted to master one before I went on to the next. I'm not saying only sell on one channel. I'm just saying master one first then go to the next one then go to the next one then go to the next one."
[11:35] "So, we're working with Instagram to be more specific with my personal brand. And I'm telling you, or I told you at the last mastermind, I was like at 2K followers, something like that. Now, now I'm close to seven."
[11:50] "Um, just growing it steadily. And how we spec specifically get clients is through daily postings to build like value or deliver value. Daily stories to deliver value and show the lifestyle that they want and then the DM strategy which is going to be the framework that I share in the end."
[12:10] "And it's literally like the 120K that we made last month is all through my DMs from an appointment setter there. Nothing else. No ads, nothing. That's just it."
[12:22] "Holy shit!"
[12:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Smoothest Sales Process"
NÚMEROS: 20% increase in closing rate
VISUAL: Três etapas numeradas: "1. Strategy Call (Zoom)", "2. Sign Agreement (PandaDoc)", "3. Payment (Stripe)", setas indicando fluxo, ícone de cartão de crédito sem cobrança imediata
[12:28] "And then as a little bonus, the sales process and and this is interesting. Um, I've seen a lot. Yeah, sure. Go ahead."
[12:36] "You do run ads to your page."
[12:38] "We do run ads to the page, but don't run ads to an offer."
[12:42] "Yeah. So, the smoothest sales process that we found that puts your leads minds at ease, and that's so interesting. Who here takes payment on the first call? Does anybody? You guys do. Okay, good."
[12:58] "I've bought a couple of uh coaching consulting programs in the past where I had to pay on the call. And it was always that feeling of I don't I don't know if my card gets declined because I don't know if there's enough money on it. Like let me think about it. I'm going to get back to you in like the next couple days."
[13:18] "So what we did to improve that and to make it way way smoother was obviously they have the strategy call, right, which is in Zoom. Then they signed the agreement through Panda Do."
[13:30] "And this is crucial because I I'm sure other software can do that too. But with Pendodoc, there is that credit card field. Okay? And as soon as they put in their credit card details, it gets automatically transferred to Stripe, but it does not charge the card."
[13:48] "And that is what you can tell them. It's like, hey, you're going to sign the agreement now, but you don't have to pay now. Okay? And and that increased our closing rate by like 20%. It's insane because people feel way more comfortable signing it and having to pay."
[14:08] "Even if if they pay tomorrow, it doesn't matter. It just makes it way smoother for them. So, the third thing is obviously they pay."
[14:15] "Yep."
[14:17] "Okay. Like obviously there's room for abuse on that. What do you what do you do to curb that?"
[14:23] "Uh what's that, sir?"
[14:25] "Hey, yeah, I I'll pay later. Cool. And then, you know, 12 weeks later on board and paid and shit."
[14:32] "Now, I mean, we we set an onboarding day, uh, like an onboarding date during that call. So, in the strategy call, as soon as they sign the agreement, it's like, 'Hey, next thing we're going to do is we set up an onboarding call.'"
[14:48] "Um, that's going to be tomorrow or the day after, right? And in that call, you're going to get access to the video training. You're going to get access to a calendar with all the Zoom links. You're going to get access to the WhatsApp group, everything you need.' Yep."
[15:05] "Is the strategy call and signing the agreement on the first call or just the signing of the agreement on the second?"
[15:12] "Great question. Um, we do it in the same call."
[15:15] "Yep. It's all one call close."
[15:18] "So, you are collecting your payment information on that call. So, that way there's no way of them like ghosting you after."
[15:25] "Correct. I mean, they can technically if they just give you a a card that they don't use and the limit is..."
[15:32] "Yeah."
[15:33] "And to be clear on that onboarding is it they they need to obviously pay in order to get access to assets. Correct."
[15:40] "Yeah."
[15:41] "Yeah. So, so the we charge the cart before the onboarding call and if we can charge it then in the onboarding call we're going to be like, 'Hey, we saw that your car get the client got declined. What's the matter there?'"
[15:55] "Right."
[15:56] "Got it. Well, thank you."
[15:58] "Yep."
[16:00] "So, you're DMing people that only follow."
[16:03] "We're DMing people that follow follow us. So, if if let's say I get a follower now, my appointment setter will DM them, 'Hey, um, thanks for the follow. Did you find me through the ad? And are you a full-time videographer?'"
[16:20] "So you get them to follow you first because like if you just call uh DM you hit like their general inbox."
[16:28] "Yeah. No, that's not going to work. No, but if they follow you then obviously you get in the um like the primary."
[16:35] "Cool."
[16:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Learning #2: Push Your Clients to Maximize Results"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Slide com título grande, subtítulo sobre accountability e acompanhamento ativo, ícone de gráfico crescente
[16:42] "So second learning push and that was what I I was saying in the beginning. Push your clients to maximize results. Okay. I mean online marketing agencies obviously we need to get results. Consultants need to get results."
[16:58] "But one specific problem with info products, and that is why that space is so fucked up, is because like most people that sell info products, they don't fucking care. They just want to sell the course."
[17:12] "And obviously the course material is great, but how many of you, hands up, have ever bought a course and didn't watch a single video or stopped after like 10."
[17:22] "Okay, nearly all of us, right? And that is why we have to push our clients to to get the results."
[17:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Client Tracking System - Monday.com"

VISUAL: Screenshot do Monday.com mostrando colunas de rastreamento: calls missed, calls attended, video training progress, last login, WhatsApp activity, deals closed, cash flow
[17:35] "And how we do that is um first of all with the WhatsApp chats that I told you about. They're not here for them to only ask us questions, but we're tracking everything they're doing. And we do that in monday.com. And I'm going to show you real quick."
[17:52] "So, let me put that away here. So, this is our um client statusport, right? Where you can see all the clients."
[18:05] "Um, and we track how many calls have they missed. We track how many calls have they attended. We track how much have they um progressed in the video training. Were they logged in in the past seven days? Were they not logged in?"
[18:22] "Like how many questions has they have they asked us in WhatsApp chats? Like when were they last active? Right? So, we track literally every single thing that they're doing."
[18:33] "We're tracking how many deals they're closing. We're tracking how many calls they have with clients. We're tracking how many projects they win, how many projects they lose. We track how much cash flow they get. We track how much uh contracted revenue they do every single month."
[18:50] "And based on that, we reach out to them. So we use the WhatsApp chat not only for them to ask us questions, but to actually be like, 'Hey, we saw that you didn't log into the video training for three days in a row. What's up?' Right?"
[19:08] "And that is a lot of work obviously, but it also produces a shit ton of amazing results. Yeah."
[19:15] "Do you have a VA track then?"
[19:18] "Uh now I have a client success manager who is full uh full-time in Switzerland."
[19:25] "Uh yeah 5k a month. That that shit changes lives."
[19:30] "How you track your cash flow? You ask them."
[19:33] "Yeah, we have a month like a link to a monthly report that they fill out and we send that into a community chat where there like there's everybody in there um and they just fill it out."
[19:47] "It's like five questions and then the ones and that's an additional thing. The ones that don't fill it out, we um go into the one-on-one chat with them and be like, 'Hey, saw that you didn't fill out the report. Here's the link again.'"
[20:05] "Two days after, 'Hey, saw you didn't still still didn't fill out the report. Here's the link again. Do that today.' But we're really pushing them to fill that shit out because otherwise there's no way to to track it."
[20:20] "What's that?"
[20:22] "It is. It is. And they have to get hold accountable because videographers are just lazy fucks. Like they they just like to do videos and that's pretty much it. But it's not just videographers. It's literally everybody."
[20:38] "Like we we have our favorite things to do and then the shit the rest we just don't do it."

[20:58] "I I can show you guys a chat afterwards."

[21:35] "Good for now."
[21:37] "Commend you on the level of account of accountability you guys are providing your clients. That's that's awesome."
[21:43] "Thank you."
[21:45] "Oh, so that guy. And by the way, as you can tell, um, up here in the in the WhatsApp group title, we we systemized the the titles as well."
[21:58] "So it's like um the camera icon means he's in the full consulting program and then this little notebook here on the right means like tells us about the status."
[22:10] "So that means the red one means he has five strikes or more. And one strike you get an example when you haven't logged in into the to the video training for seven days. Another strike when you didn't join the calls stuff like that."
[22:28] "And that makes it super easy for me because I never have to go and see that status board because I can just scroll through the WhatsApp chats and be like, 'Oh shit that guy needs a follow up from me. That guy needs to follow up from me. That guy needs to follow up from me. And these guys are all green, right?'"
[22:48] "So, this guy um my client success manager, I don't know if you guys can see it, but he had a call with him to follow up because he didn't join the calls."
[23:00] "And then it was literally just him reaching out like, 'Hey, wait, what does he say? Can you what's it? Can you give me a quick update on your current situation?' Then he gave us a little update. My client success manager was like, 'Six sounds great.' Great."
[23:18] "And then after that, nothing happened anymore. Just like me and this client success manager reaching out with voice messages, with calls, like more messages, reaching out, messages, reaching out, reaching out, changing it to five strikes here."
[23:35] "Um, reaching out, hey, join the live call. Hey, where are you? Hey, what's the what's up with the report? Hey, here's the link again. Like, literally just reaching out, reaching out, reaching out."
[23:48] "Uh, he did on Instagram and said he's going to join the calls again, but he didn't. So yeah that happens obviously as well. Good."
[24:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Learning #3: Become an Operations God"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Slide com título grande, subtítulo sobre excelência operacional, ícone de engrenagens ou sistema
[24:05] "The third learning is become an operations god. Okay. Because the big problem of online businesses is that they're always and and we all know that they're always just talking about the things that are going well."
[24:20] "Like we're making x amount of revenue and obviously it's like a catchy title. Like I I put it in the beginning of the of the presentation, right? It's a c catchy title. But then what is in the back end?"
[24:33] "And I've been a part of offers in the past where it just got screwed over because their operations was shitty as hell. Like no structure, no systems, team wasn't like on point, accountability was missing."
[24:48] "And that's exactly what we discovered. Like it's many that struggle with operations. So um be very be very honest here. Who struggles at least a little bit with operations?"
[25:00] "Multiple hands. So you guys see it's normal, okay? It's nothing to be ashamed of as long as you plan on getting better at it. That is the most important thing."
[25:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Two Ways to Fix Operations"
NÚMEROS: 8K/month + bonuses, 350K/year earning potential
VISUAL: Duas opções lado a lado: "1. Be Fucking Good At It Yourself" vs "2. Hire Someone Fucking Good At It", destaque visual na opção 2
[25:18] "So two ways to solve this issue. Number one, either you're fucking good at it and you just do it or you hire someone who is fucking good at it. These are the only two options to fix this."
[25:32] "So for me, I chose to hire and to be honest, my life has never ever been easier than that. Like this guy that I have, my operations manager, I pay him 8K a month plus bonuses if he hits KPIs."
[25:48] "So his earning potential is up to 350K. Shut up. 350K a year."
[25:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Operations Manager Responsibilities"
NÚMEROS: 8-page SOP documents
VISUAL: Screenshot de SOP detalhado mostrando: Pre-call workflow, Strategy call workflow, scripts, Loom videos embarcados, estrutura passo-a-passo extremamente detalhada
[26:00] "Um, and this guy, let me show you some examples. He's fucking crazy. Like I love what he's doing. So um, first of all, he's writing all my SOPs. Okay."
[26:12] "So, an example, the strategy call, which is our closing call. He writes down every single step of what our closers have to do, which is going to which means like the pre-all workflow."
[26:28] "I'm actually going to zoom in here a bit for the guys on the back end. Okay. So literally what you have to do before the call, how you have to record a personalized video to tell them that you're going to be the one who's going to have the call, including the video script."
[26:48] "Including like option one when they come through uh social media, option two, they came through our website in example. Um then the strategy called workflow."
[27:00] "So like the entire uh script structure and he puts that all in writing like that's literally like an eight page document or something and so in detail that it literally when we hire a new closer we can just give him this document."
[27:18] "There's looms in it too and you can see here um and everything is just documented. He does that for..."
[27:28] "Yeah. So one of my channel our challenges has been like we'll have systems like this but we find like uh adoption um of the systems is a challenge not just the creation of the system what does make sure this is the first remains..."
[27:48] "He is let's call him a fucker. He's just like if somebody doesn't follow the exact steps and he follows like he tracks everybody he's like hey you're not following the script here why hey I saw you didn't name this file correctly why right."
[28:08] "And then do you need any assistance? Do you need help? Do you don't understand?"
[28:15] "So this tight internal quality control."
[28:18] "It's the exact same thing as we do with our students, right? It's like keeping keeping them or holding them accountable in order for them to actually get the results that counts for our team members as well."
[28:33] "Is there I mean I don't know if you have the time on this, but how does do you have any idea how he goes about maintaining that quality control across the entirety of the team?"
[28:45] "I mean I'm sure he misses some things. I'm 100% sure he misses some things, but he tracks the important stuff."
[28:53] "So let's say um for this uh closing call um operation sheet right the SOP he tracks an example what is put in our CRM do they follow the note script that they have to follow like the the structure of the notes."
[29:12] "Like the the um general notes outcome of the call what's going to be the next follow-up right do they put it like that or not then it tracks is the call uh recording uploaded into the into the CRM so I can check it and do a sales training session."
[29:32] "Like the the main parts, the most important parts. I'm 100% sure he cannot track every single thing."
[29:40] "You probably know this too. It's impossible for I'm sure you agree like it's possible for him to do his job if your team didn't hold themselves accountable too."
[29:52] "And that's one of the things like step one is having a team that can hold themselves accountable. You're going to miss stuff, which is his job to come in, but he would never be able to like..."
[30:05] "If the people on the team weren't able to first hold themselves accountable, which sounds like, oh yeah, no shit. But like there's a lot of people that just like they'll look at it, get told what to do, but they don't hold themselves accountable."
[30:20] "Yeah, 100%. Agree."
[30:23] "Any more questions on that?"
[30:25] "What What would you give that title for him? Like an operations manager?"
[30:30] "Yeah, we call him the operations manager. Not sure if that's the corporately correct title, but..."
[30:38] "Start looking for somebody."
[30:40] "Yeah."
[30:42] "I think you you have one to Is it Karina? Right. So, what's your title?"
[30:48] "Chief operating officer."
[30:50] "Okay. So, it's COO."
[30:52] "Yeah."
[30:53] "Thank you."
[30:55] "I don't want to jump the gun. I don't know if you're going to tell us this, but how did you find what was..."
[31:02] "Uh the the operations manager, you mean? Um he was my closer before and super interesting like for two and a half years and he he was good but he was not that good right."
[31:18] "But it's so funny because over the years he reached out to me multiple times and was like hey um I felt like our closing process wasn't um too well written out so I wrote down an entire SOP here you go and I'm like wait that's not your job but thank you right."
[31:38] "And I never saw it it's just it's ridiculous I didn't see it. Um, but then he almost quit his job as a closer because he didn't make as much money. Obviously, he was making like 5 to 7K a month, which obviously is not that great as a closer."
[31:58] "Um, so I I said, 'Hey, instead of quitting, why don't you take over an operations role because you were doing all this shit with the SOPs and would you like to do that?' And he was like, 'Yeah, I fucking would love to do that.'"
[32:15] "And I was like, 'Wait, why did we just waste two and a half years of you closing deals or not closing deals uh while you could have been an operations manager?'"

[34:15] "But incentivizing on giving us a referral. And that doesn't just mean, hey, we're going to pay you if you send us a referral. That means giving them their dream if they send us a referral."
[34:28] "What we mean by that is our program is called the get a red for free referral program. And a red digital cinema camera. It's like a you can get those for about starting at 10 grand, 8.5, 10 grand like that. Something like that."
[34:48] "It's like the camera that every single videographer wants, okay? It's like their dream outcome, their desire. They want to own this camera. So, that's why we pretty much named our program after this."

[44:05] "Because once you focus on one thing, your life is going to be so easy. And I I heard it multiple times before. I did it. But the thing is once you actually do it, once you actually cut all the cords, life is it's so good. It's so good."
[44:25] "You wake up in the morning, you say, 'Okay, um I got my team. What do I need to do today to make us more money?' And it's not, okay, I need to do this and this and this and this and this and this and this and this."
[44:40] "It's like your your mind frees up because there's nothing else that occupies the mind. I still have that video agency where I don't do shit I I just invest like an hour or two a week in it and the the rest gets handled by my team, but it still occupies like mental space."
[45:00] "It's still in my head and I'm I'm 100% sure as soon as I cut that one off as well, as soon as somebody buys it, um that focus part is going to be even even crazier and we're getting even even faster results."

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
0 a 100K/mês em 3 meses (primeira vez)
Voltou a 0
0 a 120K/mês em 4 meses (segunda vez)
Meta: 150-180K este mês
Meta final: $1M/mês até final de 2023
Crescimento de 2K para 7K seguidores no Instagram
120K/mês apenas através de DMs do Instagram (sem ads para oferta)
Câmera Sony FS7: $10-12K
Vídeo bombeiro: 700K views, 25 novas aplicações
RED camera: $8.5-10K
Custo por seguidor: $1
20% aumento na taxa de fechamento com novo processo de pagamento
ESTRUTURA DE PRODUTOS E PREÇOS:
Video Business Academy (VBA): $5K (meta: levar a 10K/mês)
Video Agency Expert (VAE): $24K ou $2K/mês por 12 meses (meta: 50K/mês + construir time)
Money Tree Inner Circle: $48K ou $4K/mês (1-on-1 para scaling)
COMISSÕES DE REFERRAL:
VBA: $100
Programa completo: $250
Programa avançado: $500
Inner Circle: $1,000
RECOMPENSAS DO PROGRAMA "GET A RED FOR FREE":
1ª referral: AirPods + comissão
2ª-4ª: comissão padrão
5ª: iPhone
6ª-9ª: comissão padrão
10ª: Mastermind em Dubai (acomodação + viagem)
11ª-14ª: comissão padrão
15ª: Lente até $2K
16ª-19ª: comissão padrão
20ª: RED camera ($8.5K)
CUSTOS OPERACIONAIS:
Operations Manager: $8K/mês + bônus (potencial até $350K/ano)
Client Success Manager: $5K/mês
KPIs DO OPERATIONS MANAGER (9 TOTAL):
95% dos clientes atingem 10K/mês
75% enviam video testimonial
Testimonials escritos
25% de upsell de VBA para VAE
Tracking de referrals
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
1-1-1 Strategy (Alex Hormozi):
1 Target Audience: Videógrafos que querem crescer para 10K+/mês
1 Offer: Group Consulting com video training + calls + suporte
1 Sales Process: Instagram DM Strategy
Estrutura da Oferta:
Video training base (step-by-step extremo: "dar para um macaco conseguir 10K/mês")
3 weekly live calls (expandindo para 5 por clientes australianos)
7 dias/semana WhatsApp support (1-on-1)
Forçam participação ativa nas calls
Sales Process (3 etapas):
Strategy Call (Zoom)
Sign Agreement (PandaDoc - coleta cartão MAS NÃO cobra)
Payment (Stripe - cobra antes do onboarding)
Tracking de Clientes (Monday.com):
Calls missed/attended
Video training progress
Login nos últimos 7 dias
Atividade no WhatsApp
Deals fechados
Calls com clientes
Projetos ganhos/perdidos
Cash flow mensal
Contracted revenue
Sistema de strikes (5+ = status vermelho)
Sistema de WhatsApp:
Emoji de câmera = programa completo
Emoji de notebook colorido = status (verde/amarelo/vermelho)
Follow-up agressivo para não-responsivos
Monthly report obrigatório (5 perguntas)
Estrutura Operacional:
Operations Manager escreve SOPs de 8+ páginas
SOPs incluem: scripts, workflows, Loom videos
Tracking rigoroso de compliance
Quality control através de CRM notes
Ops meetings diários
Sales dashboard automatizado (5 min/dia para revisar)
16 Personalities Test:
Dado a todos novos team members
Tipos ideais: Consul, Executive, Architect
Instagram DM Strategy:
Daily posts (value)
Daily stories (lifestyle + value)
DM quando alguém segue
Ads apenas para crescer página (não para oferta)
Appointment setter faz outreach
Framework disponível via QR code
Ad Criativo que Funciona:
Nick falando sobre construir video agency
Menciona comprar RED aos 20 anos (objeto de desejo)
Legendas obrigatórias
CTA: swipe up e siga para tips grátis
Também impulsiona best performing reels
Plano para $1M/Mês (3 Steps):
Step 1: Referrals (Get a RED for Free)
Programa gamificado com pontos
Recompensas físicas + dinheiro
20 referrals = RED camera
Marcos em 1, 5, 10, 15, 20 referrals
Link pinado no WhatsApp
Mencionado em todas as calls
Follow-up quando postam wins
Step 2: Upsells
25% de VBA → VAE (KPI do ops manager)
Só possível com resultados excepcionais
Justifica investimento de $5K/mês em Client Success
Step 3: Scale com Ads
Próxima fase (ainda não implementada)
Atualmente só ads para Instagram growth
100% Success Guarantee:
Dobrar investimento em 6 meses
Ou trabalha 1-on-1 diariamente até conseguir
Incluído no contrato (gera confiança)
LIÇÕES PRINCIPAIS:
#1 - Listen to What the Successful Say:
Seguir Alex Hormozi
Implementar 1-1-1 strategy
Perguntar: "Como fazer em metade do tempo?"
#2 - Push Clients to Maximize Results:
Info products falham por falta de accountability
Tracking obsessivo de tudo
Follow-up proativo e agressivo
Direito de expulsar clientes não-compliantes (no contrato)
#3 - Become an Operations God:
Contratar Operations Manager ($8K/mês)
SOPs extremamente detalhados
Quality control rigoroso
Team que se auto-gerencia (mas com oversight)
Libera mente do founder
#4 - Simple is King, But Be Very Specific:
Não "business owners" → "videógrafos que querem 10K+/mês"
Não "consulting" → "group consulting com video training base"
Master um canal antes do próximo
#5 - FOCUS:
Levou 6 anos para aprender
Cortar side hustles, crypto, trading
Uma coisa + scale agressivo > múltiplas coisas
Libera espaço mental
Ainda vendendo video agency (6 de 7 buyers desistiram)
TÁTICAS ESPECÍFICAS:
Para Aumentar Closing Rate:
Não cobrar no momento da assinatura (+20% conversão)
PandaDoc captura cartão mas não cobra
Cobrança antes do onboarding call
Revisar contrato junto na call (3 minutos)
Não dar opção de "pensar sobre isso"
Para Accountability:
Onboarding date marcado na strategy call
Acesso só após pagamento
Strike system automático
WhatsApp title system (emoji = status)
Follow-ups em cascata
Para Referrals:
Dar objetos físicos (não só dinheiro)
Criar programa gamificado
Dar sonho deles (RED camera)
Qualificar referrals (filtro no processo)
Mention em wins
Para Operações:
Contratar alguém que já demonstrou iniciativa
Dar KPIs claros (9 total)
Bônus baseado em performance
Ops meetings diários
Dashboards que levam 5 min/dia
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Receita atual: $120K/mês
Projeção este mês: $150-180K
Meta 2023: $1M/mês
Ticket médio VBA: $5K
Ticket médio VAE: $24K ($2K/mês)
Ticket médio Inner Circle: $48K ($4K/mês)
LTV potencial por cliente: até $77K
Custo Ops Manager: $8K/mês (até $29K/ano com bônus)
Custo Client Success: $5K/mês ($60K/ano)
CAC Instagram: $1/seguidor
ROI do Client Success: Permite 25% upsell rate
DURAÇÃO: ~1 hora FORMATO: Apresentação de mastermind com Q&A ao vivo, demonstrações de tela (Monday.com, PandaDoc, Instagram ads)
